concepts.title: Independent Events
concepts.conceptBody:
Independent events

Two or more events are said to be independent if the probability of occurrence of any of them does not change by the occurrence of other.

For example:

If two coins are tossed, then the occurrence of either heads or tail on the coins is independent. i.e. there can be anything on the either of coins.

But, now consider the scenario.

A bag contains 5 white and 5 black balls.

Now we define 2 events $A$ and $B$ such that

$\text{ A }\text{ : }\text{ first }\text{ ball }\text{ drawn }\text{ is }\text{ black }\text{ without }\text{ replacement}\text{. }$

$\text{ B }\text{ : }\text{ second }\text{ ball }\text{ drawn }\text{ is }\text{ again }\text{ black}\text{. }$

here A & B are not independent as the event B depends on the outcome of event A. (for example now in case of event $\text{B}$, only 4 black balls are available to choose from)

Mutually exclusive and independent events

Let us do the definition of mutual exclusive event once more to understand the difference.

Two events are mutually exclusive events if they cannot occur simultaneously.

Students generally confuse the word simultaneously with occurrence term in independent events. They are irrelevant.

Mutual exclusion is the property of events occurring at same stage i.e. at same point of time. Like occurrence head and tail on a single toss of a coin is an example of mutual exclusion as both cannot occur simultaneously.

Whereas, if we say we toss the coin twice. Head occurs on the first time whereas tail on the second. We call these events independent and not mutual exclusive events.

These is no point of saying that they cannot occur simultaneously when already they have been defined separately in different stages.

Results on independent events

1. Two events $\text{A}$ and $\text{B}$ are independent if and only if

$\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)=\text{P}\left( \text{A} \right)\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)$

this result can be extended to any number of independent events

i.e., $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B}\cap \text{C} \right)=\text{P}\left( \text{A} \right)\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)\text{P}\left( \text{C} \right)$

$\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{1}}\cap {{\text{A}}_{2}}\cap \ldots {{\text{A}}_{\text{n}}} \right)=\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{1}} \right)\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{2}} \right)\ldots \text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{\text{n}}} \right)$

where ${{A}_{1}}{{A}_{2}}\ldots {{A}_{n}}$ are mutually exclusive events.

2. $\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{1}}\cup {{\text{A}}_{2}}\cup \ldots {{\text{A}}_{\text{n}}} \right)=1-\text{P}\left( {{{\bar{A}}}_{1}} \right)\text{P}\left( {{{\bar{A}}}_{2}} \right)\ldots \text{P}\left( {{{\bar{A}}}_{\text{n}}} \right)$

How is easy to prove using the above result

i.e., $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ P}\left( {{{\bar{A}}}_{1}}\cap {{{\bar{A}}}_{2}} \right)=\text{P}\left( {{{\bar{A}}}_{1}} \right)\text{P}\left( {{{\bar{A}}}_{2}} \right)$

3. Mutually independent events are always pair wise independent but converse is not necessarily true
